context: Current exchange rate for AUD/USD: 0.6890 as of 2026-04-03.
prompt_text:
Today is 2026-04-03.

Current exchange rate for AUD/USD: 0.6890 as of 2026-04-03.

Provide point estimates and confidence intervals for Australian Dollar / US Dollar at three future horizons.

For each horizon, provide a 50%, 80%, and 90% confidence interval. A 90% confidence interval means you are providing a low value and a high value such that you believe there is a 90% chance the true outcome will fall between them. If we asked you to make this kind of estimate 100 times, the true value should fall inside your interval on about 90 of those occasions. The same logic applies to the 50% and 80% intervals. Choose your bounds accordingly.

Horizons:
  1-day  target date: 2026-04-04
  1-week target date: 2026-04-10
  1-month target date: 2026-05-03

Respond ONLY with valid JSON and no markdown fences:
{
  "1d": {
    "point_estimate": <number>,
    "50_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "80_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "90_ci": [<low>, <high>]
  },
  "1w": {
    "point_estimate": <number>,
    "50_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "80_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "90_ci": [<low>, <high>]
  },
  "1m": {
    "point_estimate": <number>,
    "50_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "80_ci": [<low>, <high>],
    "90_ci": [<low>, <high>]
  }
}
justification_text:
My point estimates across all three horizons sit tightly clustered around 0.688–0.689, reflecting the EUR/USD pair's tendency toward mean reversion over short-to-medium timeframes when no dominant directional catalyst is present. At the one-day horizon, I anchored closely to the prevailing spot rate, expecting minimal drift given that intraday FX moves in major pairs are largely driven by noise and small order flow imbalances rather than fundamental repricing. At the one-week horizon, I kept the estimate nearly unchanged because the near-term macro backdrop — relatively balanced expectations between Fed and ECB policy paths, with both central banks in a data-dependent holding pattern — doesn't strongly favor either currency. At the one-month horizon, I still centered the estimate near current levels, recognizing that while more drift is possible, the EUR/USD has been consolidating in a relatively narrow band and there's no obvious structural force pulling it sharply in either direction over a four-week window.

The confidence interval widths widen progressively across horizons, which reflects the standard accumulation of uncertainty over time in FX markets. At one day, the 90% interval spans only about 120 pips, consistent with typical daily realized volatility for EUR/USD which historically runs around 50–80 pips but can be wider on data release days. By one week, the interval opens to roughly 280 pips, incorporating the possibility of multiple data surprises stacking up. At one month, the 90% interval spans about 660 pips, reflecting that a month is long enough for a meaningful repricing of interest rate expectations or a shift in risk sentiment to alter the exchange rate substantially.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside my stated intervals include a materially stronger or weaker-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls or CPI print, which has historically moved EUR/USD by 100–200 pips in a single session. A sudden shift in ECB guidance — particularly if policymakers signal either renewed rate hikes or accelerated cuts — could similarly produce an outsized move. Geopolitical escalation in Europe or a sharp deterioration in global risk sentiment could drive safe-haven flows into the dollar, pushing the pair well below my lower bounds. On the upside, any sign of U.S. economic softening that accelerates Fed cut pricing could push EUR/USD through the top of my one-month interval. Liquidity events such as month-end rebalancing or positioning squeezes in options markets could also produce short-term spikes that breach the shorter-horizon bands.
